description: Standard: book review with mixed assessment

input.work: 'The Last Census' — debut novel by a fictional author, set in a near-future where the US government has abandoned the decennial census, following a statistician who sets out to count the uncounted
input.assessment: Inventive premise, strong first half with sharp satirical writing. The second half becomes too earnest and loses the comedic edge. The ending is satisfying but predictable. Prose is consistently excellent.
observations: The opening chapter — the protagonist standing in an empty Census Bureau office on the day it closes — is brilliantly staged
A running joke about self-reported data vs. observed reality is the book's best recurring device
The love interest subplot feels underdeveloped and generic
Secondary characters (a conspiracy theorist, a local pastor, a data scientist) are memorable
The final 80 pages lose satirical edge and become a conventional road novel

input.assessment: Excellent seasonal menu, warm atmosphere, but slow service on a busy evening. Worth a visit.
observations: The roasted cauliflower dish was exceptional
Wine list is small but well-curated

description: European: film review at Berlin film festival

input.work: 'Stillwater and Stone' — French-language drama directed by a fictional director, screened in competition at the Berlinale
input.assessment: Visually stunning and emotionally restrained. The lead performance is extraordinary. The pace is deliberately slow, which will divide audiences. The final shot is among the most beautiful images at this year's festival.
observations: The opening 12-minute single take through a quarry is a technical marvel
The lead actor communicates more in silence than most actors do in dialogue
A subplot about the protagonist's estranged daughter adds depth but is underdeveloped
The sound design is exceptional — industrial noise becomes almost musical
The film's political subtext about labor and dignity is present but never heavy-handed
